assJWZYAAAAJ.keywords: Ubiquitous Computing,Contactless physiological sensing,Doppler radar sensing,Acoustic body-sound sensing,Wearable and mobile health (mHealth),Affective computing and biofeedback,Mindless computing,Smartphone-based point-of-care diagnostics,Skin-tone calibrated pulse oximetry,Tangible user interfaces for health
assJWZYAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Alexander Adams is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads the **Uncommon Sense Lab** and directs a research program at the intersection of *ubiquitous computing*, human–computer interaction, and digital health. His work centers on developing <u>unobtrusive sensing</u> and <u>passive intervention</u> technologies that deploy everyday devices (smartphones, wearables, low-cost sensors) to measure and support physiological and behavioral health in real-world settings. He is best known for systems that combine hardware prototyping, signal processing, and human-subject studies to create practical, low-friction tools for monitoring sleep, respiration, body sounds, affect, and substance-use behaviors.

---

## Research Areas
Adams’ research spans several tightly related areas within **ubiquitous computing** and mobile health. He has pioneered contactless physiological sensing using commodity radar and motion sensors (e.g., DoppleSleep and related demos) and developed acoustic and wearable approaches to capture non-speech body sounds for dietary, respiratory, and affective sensing (BodyBeat, Sensing non-speech body sounds). A recurring theme is the design of *mindless* or minimally demanding interventions—systems that subtly influence affect and behavior without requiring conscious effort—exemplified by EmotionCheck and his work on affective touch and peripheral breathing cues (BreathePulse). He also advances low-cost, smartphone-based diagnostic and spectrometric tools (SpectraPhone / Nutrilyzer / μ-Phone) and builds research platforms for tracking emerging behaviors such as e‑cigarette use (PuffPacket). Across these topics he emphasizes robustness and equity (for example, skin-tone–calibrated SpO2 measurement in OptoBeat), real-world deployment, and cross-disciplinary validation with clinical and behavioral measures.

---

## Notable Works
- "Dopplesleep: A contactless unobtrusive sleep sensing system using short-range doppler radar" (2015) — introduced a single-sensor, contactless approach to continuous sleep and physiological tracking using commercial radar modules and demonstrated rich sleep-related measurements in everyday environments.  
- "BodyBeat: a mobile system for sensing non-speech body sounds." (2014) — developed wearable and phone-based methods to capture and classify non-speech body sounds (eating, coughing, breathing, laughter), opening new pathways for dietary, respiratory, and behavioral monitoring.  
- "EmotionCheck: leveraging bodily signals and false feedback to regulate our emotions" (2016) — combined wearable actuation and perceptual manipulation of bodily signals to demonstrate *mindless* emotion-regulation interventions that reduce anxiety through subtle feedback.

---

## Academic Background
Adams trained as an interdisciplinary researcher in **ubiquitous computing** and human–computer interaction, and his publication record reflects long-standing engagement with top conferences and journals in HCI, mobile systems, and pervasive health. He is a tenured/tenure-track faculty member at Georgia Tech (homepage: https://www.alexandertadams.com/) and directs the Uncommon Sense Lab, which collaborates closely with clinicians and engineers on applied sensing problems. His body of work has accrued over **1,200 citations**, evidencing sustained impact on mobile sensing, wearable interventions, and low-cost diagnostics. He has mentored numerous graduate students and produced multiple open research platforms and prototypes used by the community. His projects emphasize reproducibility, real-world evaluation, and attention to fairness and accessibility in sensing technologies.
